era_context: Classical Greece at a major turning point: the end of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and the establishment of the King's Peace (also called the Peace of Antalcidas). The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II emerges as the arbiter of Greek affairs. Sparta, though weakened, remains a major power. In China, the Warring States period continues; in Italy, Rome is consolidating power in Latium.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and early Silk Road networks

events.title: King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas) ends the Corinthian War
events.description: The King's Peace, negotiated by the Persian satrap Antalcidas, ends the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and establishes a new framework for Greek-Persian relations. The peace terms, dictated by Persia, grant Persian control over Greek cities in Asia Minor and Cyprus, guarantee the autonomy of Greek city-states in Europe, and establish Sparta as the guarantor of the peace. This represents a major shift in the balance of power: Persia becomes the arbiter of Greek affairs, and Sparta, though weakened by the war, is elevated as the enforcer of Persian-backed order. The peace is unstable and will be challenged repeatedly over the next two decades.
events.certainty_note: The King's Peace is one of the best-documented events of the 4th century BCE, attested in contemporary sources (Xenophon) and confirmed by later historiographic consensus. The date of 387 BCE is secure.
cross_references: Leads to -385-002 (Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian control)
Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, which undermines the peace)

events.title: Plato travels to Sicily and meets Dionysius I
events.description: Plato, the Athenian philosopher, travels to Syracuse in Sicily to meet Dionysius I, the tyrant. This visit marks the beginning of Plato's involvement with Sicilian politics and his attempt to influence Dionysius toward philosophical governance. The visit is traditionally dated to 387 BCE, though some sources place it slightly later (c. 388 BCE). Plato's engagement with Dionysius will lead to multiple visits and attempts to educate the tyrant's son, Dionysius II, in philosophy—efforts that ultimately fail but become legendary in later accounts.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers (Book 3.18–20)

events.certainty_note: Plato's first visit to Syracuse is traditionally dated to 387 BCE, but the exact date is uncertain. The Seventh Letter, if authentic, provides first-hand testimony, but its authenticity is disputed by modern scholars. The visit is confirmed by later sources, but the precise date may be 388 or 386 BCE.
cross_references: Leads to -367-001 (Plato's second visit to Syracuse)

events.title: Sparta reasserts control over the Peloponnese
events.description: In the aftermath of the Corinthian War, Sparta uses the King's Peace as a framework to reassert its dominance over the Peloponnese. Spartan forces move to suppress or control rival cities and restore Spartan hegemony. This reassertion is enabled by the peace settlement, which grants Sparta the role of guarantor of Greek autonomy—a position Sparta interprets as justifying intervention in the affairs of other Greek states. However, this aggressive interpretation of the peace will generate resentment and resistance, particularly from Thebes.
events.certainty_note: Sparta's reassertion of control is attested in Xenophon's account, but the specific chronology of individual actions in 387 BCE versus the broader period 387–380 BCE is uncertain.
cross_references: Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, which ends Spartan hegemony)

events.title: Thebes expelled from the Boeotian League by Sparta
events.description: Following the King's Peace, Sparta moves to break up the Boeotian League, which it views as a threat to Greek autonomy (as defined by the peace). Thebes is expelled from the league, and Spartan forces occupy key Boeotian cities. This action is justified under the King's Peace's guarantee of city autonomy, but it is widely seen as Spartan aggression. The expulsion of Thebes from the league marks a turning point: it alienates Thebes from the Spartan-backed order and sets the stage for Thebes's later resistance and rise to power.
events.certainty_note: The expulsion of Thebes from the Boeotian League is well-attested in Xenophon's contemporary account. The date of 387 BCE is secure.
cross_references: Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, Thebes's revenge)

disconfirming_evidence: The King's Peace is traditionally presented as a triumph for Sparta and a stabilizing force in Greek politics. However, modern scholarship increasingly recognizes it as a fundamentally unstable arrangement that sowed the seeds of future conflict. The peace's guarantee of city autonomy, interpreted by Sparta as justifying intervention, was widely resented and led to Spartan overreach. Additionally, the precise date of Plato's first visit to Syracuse is disputed: some sources place it in 388 BCE, others in 387 BCE, and some even later. The Seventh Letter, which provides Plato's own account, is of disputed authenticity, and modern scholars are divided on whether it is a genuine work by Plato or a later forgery. The significance of Thebes's expulsion from the Boeotian League in 387 BCE specifically (versus the broader period of Spartan reassertion in the 380s BCE) is not sharply defined in sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 387 BCE is rich, particularly for the King's Peace, which is one of the best-documented events of the 4th century BCE. Xenophon's Hellenica provides a contemporary account, and later sources (Plutarch, Diodorus) confirm and elaborate on the event. However, the record is heavily weighted toward political and military history in Greece and the Eastern Mediterranean. Persian sources are sparse in surviving form. The record reflects the interests and perspectives of Greek elites, particularly Athenian and Spartan sources. Events in other regions (Italy, Egypt, the broader Mediterranean, Asia, Africa) are poorly documented or absent. The significance attributed to the King's Peace in modern historiography may reflect a bias toward viewing it as a major turning point, when it was perhaps seen as less epochal by contemporaries.

graph_edges.note: The King's Peace establishes Persian dominance over Greek affairs, which Artaxerxes II consolidates in the following years.
The King's Peace provides the framework for Sparta's reassertion of control over the Peloponnese.
The King's Peace justifies Sparta's expulsion of Thebes from the Boeotian League.
Thebes's expulsion from the Boeotian League alienates it from the Spartan-backed order and sets the stage for Thebes's later military victory at Leuctra.
Plato's first visit to Syracuse leads to his later involvement in Sicilian politics and his second visit.
